name: Taweret
description: Taweret, also spelled Taueret, Taurt, Tuat, and Tawaret, is an ancient Egyptian protective deity. She is typically depicted as a bipedal female hippopotamus with feline paws, the tail of a crocodile, and human breasts. This fearsome yet maternal appearance was meant to ward off evil spirits and protect women during pregnancy and childbirth. Taweret's hybrid form embodies the protective nature of dangerous animals, harnessing their power to safeguard the vulnerable. She was particularly revered as a patron of fertility and childbirth, ensuring the safe delivery of babies and the well-being of mothers. As a household deity, Taweret's image was frequently found on amulets, furniture, and other domestic objects, signifying her constant presence and watchful eye. Her association with the afterlife is also evident, as she was believed to assist in the regeneration of the deceased. Taweret's power stemmed from her ability to confront and overcome chaos, providing comfort and security in a world filled with uncertainty. She was a powerful symbol of protection, motherhood, and regeneration, embodying the fierce yet nurturing aspects of the divine feminine. Her influence extended from the daily lives of ordinary Egyptians to the grand narratives of the afterlife, solidifying her place as a beloved and respected deity.
subtitle: Protector of women and childbirth

keyMyths.title: Taweret's Protection
keyMyths.description: Taweret is believed to fiercely protect pregnant women and newborns from evil spirits that seek to harm them. Her image on amulets provides constant protection.

keyMyths.title: Taweret and Regeneration
keyMyths.description: In the afterlife, Taweret assists in the regeneration of the deceased, ensuring their safe passage into the next world. She helps overcome obstacles in the underworld.

keyMyths.title: Taweret and Bes
keyMyths.description: Taweret is often associated with Bes, another protector deity, and they are frequently depicted together, amplifying their protective powers. Together they ensure safety and prosperity.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson

worshipPractices.name: Household Shrines
worshipPractices.description: Small shrines dedicated to Taweret were common in Egyptian homes, particularly in areas associated with childbirth and children's bedrooms. These shrines often contained amulets and figurines of Taweret, believed to offer protection to the family.

worshipPractices.name: Amulets
worshipPractices.description: Amulets depicting Taweret were worn by pregnant women and new mothers to ensure a safe pregnancy and delivery. These amulets were typically made of faience or other materials and were believed to transfer Taweret's protective powers to the wearer.

festivals.name: Birth Celebrations
festivals.description: While no specific festivals dedicated solely to Taweret are known, she was prominently invoked and celebrated during childbirth. Offerings and prayers were made to her to ensure the safe delivery and well-being of both mother and child.

cultural.modernLegacy: Taweret's image persists in modern neo-pagan and Kemetic reconstructionist religious practices. She is also a popular figure in contemporary art and literature, often symbolizing protection, motherhood, and the fierce defense of the vulnerable. Her unique appearance continues to fascinate and inspire.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The exact origins of Taweret are somewhat obscure, with her worship dating back to the Old Kingdom period (c. 2686–2181 BCE). Unlike many other Egyptian deities, she doesn't have a clear-cut origin myth or parentage readily available in surviving texts. Her emergence seems to stem from the primal fears and anxieties surrounding childbirth and the need for a powerful protector. The hippopotamus, a fiercely protective animal in the Nile, likely served as the initial inspiration for her form. Over time, other protective animalistic features like feline paws and a crocodile tail were added, solidifying her role as a formidable guardian against evil forces. Her association with the northern sky, specifically the circumpolar stars, further elevated her status, connecting her to the eternal and unchanging realm of the cosmos. This association reinforced her image as a constant and reliable protector.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Taweret does not feature prominently in major Egyptian myths in the same way as deities like Isis or Osiris, her protective role is consistently emphasized in spells, incantations, and funerary texts. She is often depicted alongside other protective deities, such as Bes, reinforcing her function as a guardian against malevolent spirits and dangers associated with childbirth. In the Book of the Dead, Taweret is sometimes mentioned as one of the deities who assist the deceased in their journey through the afterlife, offering protection against the perils of the underworld. Her image was frequently inscribed on beds, chairs, and other household items, further emphasizing her role as a constant presence in the lives of ordinary Egyptians. These depictions served as a reminder of her protective powers and a reassurance against the ever-present threats of disease, misfortune, and evil spirits.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Taweret was primarily worshipped in homes, where she was considered a household deity. Small shrines and amulets dedicated to her were common, particularly in areas associated with childbirth and children's bedrooms. Pregnant women and new mothers frequently wore amulets depicting Taweret to ensure a safe pregnancy and delivery. These amulets were believed to transfer her protective powers to the wearer. While there were no major temples dedicated solely to Taweret, she was often included in the pantheon of deities honored in larger temples. Evidence suggests that she was particularly venerated in Thebes and Deir el-Medina, where many artisans and laborers resided. Her popularity stemmed from her approachable nature and her direct involvement in the everyday lives of ordinary Egyptians, offering protection against the tangible threats they faced.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Taweret's legacy extends beyond ancient Egypt, influencing later cultures and continuing to resonate in modern times. Her image has been found on artifacts throughout the Mediterranean world, demonstrating her appeal to diverse populations. In modern times, Taweret has experienced a resurgence of interest, particularly within neo-pagan and Kemetic reconstructionist religious movements. She is often invoked as a protector of women, children, and families, embodying strength, resilience, and maternal care. Her unique and striking appearance has also made her a popular figure in contemporary art, literature, and popular culture, often symbolizing the fierce defense of the vulnerable and the power of female strength. Her enduring appeal lies in her ability to represent both the fearsome and the nurturing aspects of motherhood and protection.

shortDescription: Egyptian protective deity, depicted as a hippopotamus-like creature, guarding women during pregnancy and childbirth, and warding off evil.
